summary: Cantrill follows up on a months-delayed promise to release DTrace diffs for Ruby on Rails with is-enabled probes. He uses the occasion to enthusiastically promote Brendan Gregg's Helper Monkey tool, which brings DTrace-style debugging capabilities to JavaScript. The post highlights the desperate state of JavaScript debugging at the time and positions Helper Monkey as a transformative advancement for AJAX development.
key_points: Released Ruby 1.8.2 DTrace diffs with is-enabled probes after months of delay
Helper Monkey brings DTrace debugging capabilities to JavaScript
JavaScript was becoming critically important but remained poorly understood and difficult to debug
The tool represents a major leap forward for web development debugging
money_quotes: Brendan's work is an indisputable quantum leap for what has become one of the most important, one of the most misunderstood, and certainly one of the most undebuggable platforms on the planet: JavaScript.
If you do anything AJAX-ian that's evenly vaguely performance or footprint sensitive, you're wasting your time if you're not using Helper Monkey.

Adam's line was that Brendan had just propelled JavaScript debugging forward by '100,000 years' – which is not to say that Helper Monkey is like debugging in the 1021st century, but rather that debugging JavaScript without Helper Monkey is like debugging in the late Pleistocene.
key_insight: JavaScript had become critically important infrastructure while remaining essentially impossible to debug properly, making tools like Helper Monkey that bring serious observability to the platform genuinely transformative.
